direction.title: Formal-system-adaptive conjecture verification for multi-agent discovery systems
direction.rationale: All branches (A–H) inherit the assumption of a fixed formal environment (Lean, linear algebra, group theory) for verification, blocking the ability to adapt to new mathematical domains. This shared residual gap is the most pressing bottleneck—the field has decentralized conjecture generation and proof attempts but remains tethered to a single formal system. Solving it would enable multi-agent systems to operate across arbitrary formalisms without manual reconfiguration.
direction.meta_gap: The multi-agent system does not establish a formal-system-adaptive conjecture verification property; it inherits from all ancestors the assumption that a fixed formal environment (Lean, linear algebra, group theory) is available for verification or evaluation.
direction.shared_mechanism: All branches assume a fixed formal system (e.g., Lean, linear algebra axioms) for proof verification, preventing the system from generalizing to domains where no such pre-built environment exists.
direction.non_obvious_property: Property: Formal-system-agnostic conjecture verification that does not require a pre-defined proof assistant. Obvious composition that fails: Multi-agent RL + a formal proof assistant (e.g., Lean Copilot + Aristotle). This fails because the formal assistant is hard-coded; the system cannot switch to a different logic or axiom set without retraining. Why prior work has not formalized this property: Prior work treats formal verification as a fixed infrastructure (e.g., Lean, group theory axioms) rather than a learnable component of the discovery loop.

per_trend_predictions.direction: Decentralized emergent formal verification through adversarial proof generation and dynamic axiom discovery in multi-agent discovery systems
per_trend_predictions.rationale: Current multi-agent discovery systems rely on a fixed formal verifier (e.g., Lean) which limits adaptability to new domains. By replacing the static verifier with a decentralized adversarial process where agents rotate roles as provers and verifiers, the system can adapt its formal environment (axioms, inference rules) based on collective experience, enabling generalization to domains without pre-built formal systems.
per_trend_predictions.builds_on: Discovering mathematical concepts through a multi-agent system
per_trend_predictions.persistent_gap: The multi-agent system does not establish a formal-system-adaptive conjecture verification property; it inherits from all ancestors the assumption that a fixed formal environment (Lean, linear algebra, group theory) is available for verification or evaluation.
per_trend_predictions.why_still_open: Prior approaches either assume a fixed verifier or use a single centralized verifier; no prior work has demonstrated a decentralized, adaptive verification mechanism that emerges from agent interactions without a pre-specified formal system.
per_trend_predictions.bridge_keep: Multi-agent structure with sharing of data distribution, conjecture generation, and proof attempts
per_trend_predictions.bridge_remove: Fixed formal verification environment (e.g., Lean) and the assumption that all proofs must be checked against a static set of rules
per_trend_predictions.bridge_add: Introduce a decentralized adversarial verification loop: agents are randomly assigned roles as prover and verifier for each conjecture. The prover attempts to construct a proof; the verifier attempts to find a counterexample or flaw. The roles rotate, and agents are rewarded based on success (truthfulness). Additionally, agents can propose new inference rules or axioms; these are accepted only if they survive multiple adversarial verification rounds and are not contradicted by existing high-confidence statements. The system maintains a dynamic 'trustworthiness' score for each inference rule based on its usage and lack of counterexamples, effectively creating an adaptive formal system that grows with the community's collective proof activity.
per_trend_predictions.non_obvious_property: Property: Verification soundness arises from a game-theoretic equilibrium where for any invalid proof, there exists an incentive for some agent to refute it, ensuring that the set of accepted theorems is closed under a stability condition. Why prior work cannot exploit it: Prior methods (e.g., Lean's kernel, single-agent prover-verifier games like GPT-f) rely on a fixed formal system or a central discriminator; they do not allow dynamic addition of inference rules based on collective consensus, nor do they guarantee incentive compatibility without centralized rewards. Why composition fails: A naive composition 'adversarial training + multi-agent theorem proving' would treat prover and verifier as static neural networks with fixed objective; our mechanism's load-bearing piece is the rotating role assignment and dynamic rule adoption, which composition cannot recover because the rules emerge from multi-agent consensus rather than being pre-defined.
per_trend_predictions.obvious_composition_check: Adversarial training + multi-agent theorem proving. This description omits the load-bearing pieces: (i) agents dynamically propose and accept new inference rules based on adversarial survival, and (ii) the incentive structure ensures soundness without a central verifier. Statically composing an adversarial network with a multi-agent proof generator does not yield an adaptive formal system or game-theoretic soundness guarantee.
per_trend_predictions.forest_meta_gap: The multi-agent system does not establish a formal-system-adaptive conjecture verification property; it inherits from all ancestors the assumption that a fixed formal environment (Lean, linear algebra, group theory) is available for verification or evaluation.
per_trend_predictions.forest_shared_mechanism: All branches assume a fixed formal system (e.g., Lean, linear algebra axioms) for proof verification, preventing the system from generalizing to domains where no such pre-built environment exists.
